title: Chapter 11: The Lock
content: The plan came to Jane not all at once but in pieces, the way a garment assembles itself on the dress form — pins first, then basting, then the slow architecture of seam meeting seam. She sat on the edge of her bed in the small room Thornfield assigned to its pattern-makers, hands clasped between her knees, and stared at the wall as though the plaster might wire itself into a schematic.
The electronic lock. Four digits. A keypad worn smooth by one person's fingers, day after day, night after night. Grace Poole's fingers.

Grace, who brought meals down the corridor at precise intervals. Grace, who held a ring of keys on her belt that jingled when she walked, though Jane had never once seen her use them for anything but the linen closet and the kitchen stores. The basement door required no key — just the code, punched and forgotten. Grace knew it. Grace always knew it.
And Grace had a crack in her.

Jane had noticed it the way she noticed everything about fabric — not consciously at first, but in the accumulated evidence of small gestures. The way Grace's fingers twitched when someone mentioned selvedge. The way her thumb and forefinger pressed together, rubbing an invisible thread, whenever the archive's vintage pieces were discussed. The way she had looked — really looked — at the Dior sample Jane carried through the corridor last week, her eyes tracking the construction with the involuntary hunger of a professional surveying good work.

Grace Poole had been a seamstress. Before Thornfield, before this strange custodial role that kept her tethered to the atelier's margins, she had built garments with her hands. And her hands remembered.
There was a crack there. Jane just had to wedge it open.

She found Adèle in the archive, as she'd expected. The girl had made the back corner her study — textbooks spread across the cutting table, a half-finished toile pinned to a miniature form beside her.
"Mademoiselle Eyre." Adèle straightened. "I was working on the dart placement. I think I finally understand what you meant about the bust point migrating —"
"It can wait." Jane pulled a stool to the table and sat. "I need your help with something."

Adèle's eyes widened. Jane never asked for help. Jane was the one who gave it, who corrected and guided and occasionally rearranged Adèle's entire approach to a garment with three economical words. For Jane to request assistance was atmospheric — a shift in pressure.

"I want to spend time with Mrs. Poole," Jane said. "After hours, when the atelier is quiet. She has knowledge about vintage construction that I can't find in any book — hand-finishing techniques, boning methods from the fifties. She was a professional before she came here."
Adèle nodded slowly. She had sensed the seamstress in Grace too, the way children sense sadness — not by analysis but by the animal certainty of proximity.

"But I can't approach her directly," Jane continued, and this was where the lie began — where the truth bent, like fabric eased into a curve. "She's private. Suspicious of anyone who wants something. If I come asking questions, she'll shut down. But if you bring her a problem — a genuine construction problem — something that requires her hands to solve…"
"You want me to ask her for help," Adèle said.
"Bring her something from the archive. Something complex enough that she'll need to pick it up, feel it, work through it with you. Something that will remind her of what she used to do."

Adèle was quiet for a moment. In that silence, Jane felt the weight of what she was doing — using this girl's genuine curiosity as a tool. The lie was close enough to the truth to be invisible, and that made it worse.
"The Schiaparelli-inspired piece," Adèle said. "From the 1954 collection. The structured peplum with the internal boning — I've been wanting to understand that boning for weeks."
"Perfect." Jane's voice didn't waver. "Take it to her after dinner. Tell her you're stuck. Tell her you need to understand how the channels are structured. Make her show you."
Adèle's fingers drummed the table — a nervous habit she'd picked up from watching Jane think. "And while she's showing me?"
"I'll be in the archive. Researching."

The lie sat between them like a basted seam — holding everything together, but visible if you looked closely. Adèle didn't look closely. She was already reaching for the garment bag, already invested in the construction problem, already believing that Jane Eyre wanted nothing more than to learn.

They moved after dinner, when the atelier emptied of its day staff and the corridors settled into the hush of a building holding its breath. Jane watched from the staircase as Adèle carried the garment bag to the small sitting room where Grace Poole spent her evenings — a room Jane had never entered, a room that belonged to Thornfield's hidden architecture.
Adèle knocked. The door opened. Grace's silhouette filled the frame — broad-shouldered, solid, the shape of a woman who had learned to take up space as a form of authority.
"Mrs. Poole? I'm sorry to bother you. I have this piece from the archive, and I cannot for the life of me understand the boning structure, and Mademoiselle Eyre said you might—"
"I don't do that anymore."
The words came flat, rehearsed, a line Grace had delivered so many times it had worn smooth. Jane pressed herself against the staircase wall and listened.

"Please," Adèle said, and her voice carried that particular quality of youthful earnestness that no adult defense could fully repel. "Just one look. I've been staring at it for days and I can't figure out how the channels curve at the waist. It's like they're doing something impossible."
Silence. Then the sound of a zipper — Adèle opening the garment bag.
"I really can't—"
"See how the boning goes through here? But then it just… stops. There has to be a second channel, but I can't find where it branches."
A pause. A long one. Then, so quietly Jane almost missed it: "May I?"
The sound of fabric being lifted, handled, turned. The whisper of a seam being traced.

"There," Grace said. Her voice had changed — softer, more precise, the voice of a craftswoman identifying a problem. "You're looking for a straight channel, but it's curved. See? The boning follows the rib cage. It's not structural, it's anatomical. Schiaparelli built for the skeleton, not the skin."
"Show me?"

A rustle. Grace was sitting down. Grace was spreading the garment across her lap. Grace was — and Jane heard it clearly now — picking up a seam ripper from the side table, the one that was always there, always within reach, as though Grace kept her old tools the way amputees kept phantom limbs.
Jane moved.

The corridor was longer than she remembered. Or perhaps time had stretched, each second pulled thin by the weight of what she was about to do. The floor was stone — old stone, pre-dating the atelier's renovation — and her soft-soled flats made no sound against it. She had changed into them hours ago, planning for this exact passage.

The basement door appeared at the corridor's end: the modern keypad mounted at shoulder height, the reinforced frame, the telltale red light of a locked system waiting for its four-digit offering. The air down here was different — cooler, stripped of the atelier's familiar cocktail of sizing and steam. It smelled of stone and distance, of spaces not meant to be entered.
Four digits. 10,000 possible combinations, and she had minutes — maybe three, maybe five if Adèle could keep Grace engaged.
Edward's birthday. She'd found it on a bottle of vintage Dom Pérignon in the archive: *Pour mon anniversaire, 07-12.* 0-7-1-2. The pad emitted a flat, dismissive tone. Red.
The year Thornfield was founded — 2011, from the incorporation documents in Edward's office. 2-0-1-1. Red again. The same dead tone.
Sweat gathered along her palms. She wiped them on her trousers and stared at the keypad.
A laugh — not heard now, but remembered. That dark, climbing laugh that had chased her through the upper floors on her third night. The laugh of the woman behind the lock.
Think like her.

The HELP codes. She had found them stitched into the archive's antique garments — tiny Morse sequences hidden in the seams, dots and dashes rendered in contrasting thread. *H*: four dots. *E*: one dot. *L*: dot-dash-dot-dot. *P*: dot-dash-dash-dot. A seamstress's cry for assistance, encoded in the only language she had.
If the mind that hid HELP in hand-stitching used the same logic for the lock that kept her prisoner, then the pattern was the key.
H-E-L-P in alphabetical positions: 8-5-12-16. Too many digits. First four: 8-5-1-2. Red.
The number of Morse components per letter. H: 4. E: 1. L: 4. P: 4. 4-1-4-4. Red.
The electronic lock beeped its refusal, and the sound seemed too loud in the quiet corridor. Jane pressed her back to the wall and breathed. Empty. The stone held its silence.

Think like a seamstress, not a cryptographer. A seamstress didn't count components. She felt rhythm. The pattern lived in her fingers, not her mind — the same way Jane's fingers knew how to turn a curve before her brain calculated the geometry.
Her fingers were moving without her permission, tracing patterns against her thigh — the same unconscious gesture she'd seen in Grace Poole. Dot-dot-dot-dot. Dot. Dot-dash-dot-dot. Dot-dash-dash-dot.

Not the letter-by-letter breakdown. The PATTERN of the whole word, reduced to its simplest stitch-count. HELP in Morse, laid end to end: four dots, then one dot, then four signals, then four signals. No — that was still 4-1-4-4. Already tried.

Unless it wasn't about breaking the word into letters at all. Unless it was about the complete rhythmic shape. Four dots for H. Then across the whole of E-L-P: four dots and four dashes. No — that was wrong too. She was spiraling, and the clock in her head was running out—

The number arrived not as logic but as proprioception — her fingers knowing the answer the way they knew a cutting line, by feel rather than thought. Four dots in H. The pattern of E: one signal. L: four signals. P: four signals. That was 4-1-4-4 again, already failed. But Bertha wouldn't think in decomposition. She'd think in the total rhythmic weight of each letter, the way a seamstress counts the threads per centimeter, not the individual fibers.
H: 4 dots, 0 dashes. Total: 4. E: 1 dot, 0 dashes. But that gave 1, and she'd already tried 4-1-4-4.
Her fingers didn't care what her mind reasoned. They typed 4-4-4-5 before she could stop them.
Each depression a small, satisfying click. And for one suspended moment the red light held, the corridor breathed, the whole of Thornfield seemed to pause around her like a held stitch.
Then: green. A single, pure tone, high and clear. The lock disengaged with a solid, mechanical click — the sound of a bolt withdrawing, the sound of a boundary dissolving.
Jane pushed the door open.
The basement corridor swallowed her.

The air changed first — clean, cold, artificially maintained. Climate control. Preservation-grade. The kind of environment Thornfield used for its most fragile textiles, where temperature and humidity were held to tolerances tighter than a couture seam. The chill settled on her arms and raised the fine hair at her nape. She breathed in: filtered air, the faint mineral scent of stone, and beneath it something else — something organic and human, the ghost of meals eaten in captivity, of skin and sweat and existence persisted in a space not meant for living.

The corridor stretched ahead, flanked by storage. Fabric bales wrapped in archival tissue, their shapes cylindrical and cryptic under dim fluorescent strips. Garment racks on rolling wheels, their contents shrouded in protective covers, dress forms standing like sentinels in the half-light. The hum of preservation systems — fans, compressors, the subtle vibration of machinery maintaining the conditions that kept old silk from shattering and ancient wool from moth and mold.
Jane moved forward. Her flats made no sound on the sealed concrete floor. The corridor was perhaps thirty meters long, and at its end: another door.
This one was different. Reinforced steel, not wood. A modern installation in an ancient space — heavier, more deliberate. This door was not about preservation. This door was about containment.
And it had a window. Small, rectangular, set at eye level. Reinforced glass with a wire mesh core — the kind used in places where the people on one side might want to break through to the other.

Jane approached. Her heartbeat filled the corridor, amplified and returned by the close walls. She stopped six inches from the glass and leaned forward, cupping her hands around the window, blocking the corridor's light with her body.

Darkness on the other side. Not absolute — a faint light, perhaps from a window beyond, or a low-wattage bulb. The space was larger than she'd expected, the dimensions of a room rather than a cell, though she could not make out its edges.
Movement. A shift in the darkness. The sense of a large shape rearranging itself.
Then, close — terribly close, inches from the other side of the glass — a hand.

It appeared the way a garment appears when the lights come up: suddenly, completely. A woman's hand. Dark skin, the rich deep brown of mahogany, skin that had never seen the Loire Valley sun. Long fingers — pianist's fingers, or seamstress's fingers — calloused at the tips in the specific pattern of needlework: the pad of the index finger, the side of the middle finger, the small hardened ridge where a thimble had pressed for years. A thin scar ran across the knuckles, white against the dark skin, old and healed and permanent.
The hand did not pound. It did not wave. It did not claw or scratch or beat against the glass.

It pressed. Gentle. Deliberate. The palm flat against the window, the fingers spread wide, as though reaching for something just beyond grasp. Or as though confirming: there is someone on the other side. There is someone who sees.

Jane pressed her own hand to the glass. Her pale fingers aligned — roughly, imperfectly — with the dark ones on the other side. The barrier was cold against her palm. Centimeters of reinforced glass and wire mesh, and through it nothing but silence and the shape of a hand.
The ghost was real.
She had a hand. She had callouses from needlework. She had a scar. She was a woman behind a door, pressing her palm to the glass to say: *I'm here.*

Jane stood in the cold corridor, her hand raised to meet a stranger's, and understood with absolute certainty that everything she thought she knew about Thornfield was a garment worn inside out — all the seams exposed, all the construction visible, and the real surface hidden against the skin.
She did not knock. She did not call out. She held her hand where it was, and on the other side of the glass, the hand held steady too.

The basement hummed. The preservation systems cycled. Somewhere above them, Grace Poole was demonstrating a boning channel to Adèle Varens, her fingers tracing the curve of a seam she would never again call her own.
And in the dark room at the end of the corridor, a woman waited — her palm to the glass, her silence a language all its own, one door away from everything Jane had come to Thornfield to find.
